chunks.text: The 4 August 2020 Beirut port explosion (~2,750 tonnes ammonium nitrate detonating after a warehouse fire) is the modern reference incident for civilian large-scale blast mass casualty: 218 dead, ~7,000 injured, ~300,000 displaced, hospitals within 2 km direct-blasted out of service. Operational lessons codified by Lebanese Red Cross and WHO post-incident review: (1) primary blast lung injury manifests minutes-to-hours post-exposure with progressive hypoxia even in patients ambulatory at scene — mandatory 6-hour observation for all blast-exposed casualties even without external injury; (2) tertiary blast injuries (impact from being thrown) dominated trauma load — penetrating glass shrapnel from windows up to 2 km away; (3) hospital surge collapse when downwind hospitals self-presented with their own staff casualties forced cross-border evacuation to Damascus, Tel Aviv, Cyprus; (4) ammonium nitrate residue PPE: dust mask insufficient, full-face respirator with P100 needed for rescue work in debris cloud first 48 hours, post-detonation NOx vapors persist; (5) communication failure when cellular collapsed forced ham-radio coordination — confirms LoRa / Meshtastic mesh as legitimate primary, not backup. EmergencyNet anomaly_detector RED_SURGE + BURN_CLUSTER + RESP_CLUSTER co-firing within geographic 500m radius matches the Beirut signature.

The 6 February 2023 Kahramanmaraş earthquake sequence (Mw 7.8 + Mw 7.5, ~59,000 dead Turkey + Syria, ~120,000 injured) is the most recent large-scale international USAR mobilization and reshaped INSARAG response doctrine. Operational lessons: (1) 'pancake collapse' from soft-story residential buildings produced extended void survival — documented extractions at 261 hours (Hatay province), pushing INSARAG planning horizons beyond the historical 72-hour rule; (2) crush syndrome dominated post-rescue mortality — IV fluid pre-extrication (3-6L isotonic crystalloid) before releasing compression became standard rather than optional; (3) hypothermia in winter conditions with prolonged extrication required active rewarming before triage decisions, otherwise vital signs misled toward over-triage to BLACK; (4) cross-border coordination Turkey/Syria complicated by political control of border crossings — humanitarian corridors required 3+ days to negotiate, increasing preventable mortality in northwest Syria; (5) 88 international USAR teams deployed under INSARAG, demonstrating value of pre-credentialed Heavy/Medium/Light typing — teams arriving without classification spent days unassigned. EmergencyNet relevance: extended void survival validates continuous patient roster persistence in chroma_db, not 24-hour purges.

The 2022-ongoing Russian invasion of Ukraine has produced the largest dataset of modern conventional war wounds in civilian-adjacent populations since Vietnam, with substantial implications for civilian MCI doctrine where IED / artillery / drone-dropped munitions affect non-combatants. Documented patterns from Ukrainian Ministry of Health and TCCC working group: (1) extremity injuries dominate trauma load — tourniquets (CAT, SOFTT-W) the single highest-impact intervention, taught at the bystander level via 'Domedik' and Stop the Bleed campaigns; documented 90%+ survival when tourniquet applied <5 min vs <30% if delayed >15 min; (2) blast lung from sub-munition / drone-dropped explosives requires high index of suspicion in patients without external injury — SpO2 monitoring 6+ hours post-incident; (3) tympanic membrane rupture as a sentinel sign of close-range blast (within 2-3 m) → mandate evaluation for occult abdominal hemorrhage and blast lung; (4) 'mass casualty' redefined for protracted conflict: continuous moderate-volume incidents over weeks/months degrade hospital systems differently than single peaks — staff burnout, supply attrition, infrastructure damage compound. EmergencyNet Q12 (close-range blast) and Q1 (active bleeding) interact in ways that justify dual-flag triage logic.

The 22 May 2017 Manchester Arena bombing (Ariana Grande concert, 22 dead + perpetrator, 1,017 injured) established UK doctrine for crowded-place MCI and remains the reference for secondary device search protocols. Key operational lessons from the Kerslake Report and HART (Hazardous Area Response Team) review: (1) initial triage was performed in the foyer 'sterile area' before formal scene declaration — this was both necessary (penetrating injuries deteriorate fast) and risky (secondary device potential); modern doctrine: triage at 'warm zone' edge with threat-suppressed evacuation, not full clearance; (2) bolt-cutter / shrapnel pattern (TATP-based device with metal pieces) caused predominantly extremity, abdominal, and pelvic penetrating injury — pelvic binders and tourniquets used early; (3) communications failure between police, ambulance, and HART — three radio networks couldn't speak to each other, shaping post-2017 investment in Joint Emergency Services Interoperability Programme (JESIP); (4) family reunification at hospital reception was psychologically and operationally critical — designating dedicated reunification leads early prevented secondary chaos. EmergencyNet GeoFence + Meshtastic broadcast model directly addresses the JESIP communication gap with a single mesh visible to all responders.

The 13 November 2015 Paris attacks (Bataclan + Stade de France + restaurants, 130 dead + 7 perpetrators, 413 injured) reshaped European multi-site terror MCI doctrine. Operational lessons from SAMU de Paris and Préfecture de Police review: (1) multi-site attacks fragment ambulance dispatch — Paris ran 7 simultaneous incident sites, exhausting first-line SMUR teams within 90 minutes; reserve activation must trigger at first-incident, not at confirmation of multi-site; (2) damage-control resuscitation at scene (whole blood, tranexamic acid <1h, permissive hypotension) became civilian standard — Paris hospitals reported 90%+ survival for patients reaching emergency surgery alive, but 20+ minute scene-to-surgery delays for the first wave; (3) hostage incidents (Bataclan, 90+ casualties) require coordination between RAID police entry and emergency medical extraction — 'tactical medicine' embedded with police became EU standard post-2015; (4) blood bank surge: Paris depleted regional reserves within 6 hours, drove EU-wide adoption of pre-positioned cold-chain blood at incident command vehicles. EmergencyNet implication: simultaneous multi-zone GeoFence triggers should escalate command tier without waiting for cross-zone confirmation.

The 19 September 2017 Puebla earthquake (Mw 7.1, ~370 dead, ~6,000 injured), striking on the 32nd anniversary of the 1985 Mexico City quake (~10,000+ dead), tested updated Mexican Civil Protection doctrine and produced specific lessons for dense-urban earthquake MCI. Operational features: (1) 'Topos' civilian rescue brigades — voluntary teams credentialed since 1985 — perform rapid initial USAR before formal Heavy teams arrive, but require formal triage hand-off protocols to avoid casualty re-counting; (2) school collapse (Enrique Rébsamen) drove pediatric mass-casualty refinement: JumpSTART scoring at chaotic scene, family identification with photo badges pre-positioned at Mexican schools post-1985, and dedicated pediatric receiving hospitals reduce intermingling adult/child triage queues; (3) seismically amplified soft-soil zones (Lake Texcoco basin) produce non-uniform building damage even within blocks — geo-fenced rescue priorities should weight by amplification map, not radius from epicenter; (4) social media mapping ('Verificado19s') outperformed official sources for first 12 hours — informal data flow into formal command needs structured ingestion. EmergencyNet relevance: GeoJSON polygon zones already accommodate amplification metadata via ZONE_QUAKE properties.

The 12 January 2010 Haiti earthquake (Mw 7.0, ~160,000-220,000 dead, ~300,000 injured) remains the largest international medical surge mobilization in modern history and reshaped WHO Emergency Medical Team (EMT) classification. Operational lessons: (1) host-country medical infrastructure was directly destroyed — 60% of Port-au-Prince health facilities damaged or unusable, including the General Hospital; this drove the EMT Type 1/2/3 typing standard so deploying teams arrive self-sufficient (water, power, surgical capability) and don't burden local resources; (2) crush syndrome epidemic — estimated 2-5% of casualties developed acute kidney injury, requiring renal replacement therapy that didn't exist in Haiti pre-quake; mobile dialysis units pre-positioned with EMT Type 3 became standard; (3) post-disaster cholera outbreak (October 2010, ~10,000 deaths over years) introduced by UN peacekeepers' contaminated sewage — drove WHO 2014 standard for sanitation pre-deployment screening of all EMT teams; (4) airport saturation — Toussaint Louverture single runway became bottleneck, forcing US military takeover of air traffic for 3 weeks. EmergencyNet implication: extended-deployment context where local hospital capacity is ZERO, not just overwhelmed.

The 2019-2020 Australian 'Black Summer' bushfires (~33 dead direct + ~417 estimated smoke-related deaths, ~3,000 homes destroyed, ~18.6 million hectares burned) established modern doctrine for smoke-MCI and large-scale evacuation. Operational lessons from NSW/VIC RFS and Australian Medical Assistance Teams (AUSMAT): (1) PM2.5 levels in Sydney exceeded WHO 24h limit by 11x for weeks — emergency departments saw 20-40% surge in respiratory presentations (asthma, COPD, cardiac), pediatric and elderly disproportionate; (2) wildfire smoke contains CO + cyanide-precursor compounds + PM2.5 + ozone — N95 useful for particulates but inadequate for gas; vulnerable populations (CPAP-dependent, oxygen-dependent home patients) require evacuation, not shelter-in-place; (3) maritime evacuation (Mallacoota, ~4,000 evacuated by HMAS Choules) demonstrated naval support for civilian evacuation when road corridors burn over — recurring need in coastal communities; (4) 'CodeRED' phone alert system saturated cellular networks during emergency — recurring lesson driving low-bandwidth alert mesh investment. EmergencyNet RESP_CLUSTER + smoke-zone geo-fence aligns directly with this scenario; LoRa mesh broadcast works where cellular saturated.

The 22 February 2011 Christchurch earthquake (Mw 6.3, 185 dead, ~6,800 injured) demonstrated unique liquefaction-driven mass-casualty patterns and informed New Zealand and Pacific Rim earthquake doctrine. Operational lessons: (1) liquefaction-driven building tilting (CTV building, Pyne Gould Centre) produced casualty patterns different from pancake collapse — survivors trapped in 'lean spaces' rather than crush voids, requiring different USAR rescue technique; (2) urban search compounded by ongoing aftershocks Mw 5+ for months — secondary injuries to rescuers and survivors; rotation discipline (no team in damaged building >30 minutes) became standard; (3) CTV building collapse killed 115 — highlighted soft-story commercial building risk in seismic zones, drove NZ post-2011 building code revision and informed parallel revisions in Japan, Taiwan; (4) 'Student Volunteer Army' (10,000+ Canterbury University students) demonstrated civilian volunteer surge but required coordination through formal command — informal teams without check-in created hazard accountability gaps; (5) sewage system failure → norovirus / gastroenteritis surge 2-3 weeks post-quake, requiring portable sanitation as core EMT supply. EmergencyNet relevance: post-quake disease cluster anomaly detection (RESP_CLUSTER tag for respiratory + GI cluster).

The 2014-2016 West Africa Ebola outbreak (~28,600 cases, ~11,300 deaths, primarily Guinea/Liberia/Sierra Leone) reshaped infectious disease MCI doctrine in resource-limited settings. Operational lessons codified by WHO and MSF: (1) field triage requires 'no-touch' assessment — temperature scan + verbal screening from 1m distance before any contact; case definition (fever + epidemiological link OR hemorrhagic signs) determines isolation tier; (2) PPE protocol distinguished low-risk (regular mask + gown, screening) from high-risk (full Tyvek + N95 + face shield + double glove + boot covers, patient care) — cross-contamination during doffing was the dominant healthcare worker infection route, drove the 'buddy-supervised doffing' standard; (3) ambulance contamination management: dedicated Ebola transport units, single-use disposable interiors when possible, chlorine spray decon between patients; (4) burial teams as MCI front-line — body fluid contagion at funerals was the dominant community transmission vector in West Africa; (5) Community engagement with local leaders drove case-finding — purely top-down approaches failed in zones with low trust in government. EmergencyNet relevance: anomaly_detector RESP_CLUSTER with febrile pattern in geographic cluster signals possible outbreak — fundamentally different response than trauma cluster.

Saudi Arabian Hajj has produced multiple mass crowd disasters (2015 Mina stampede ~2,400 dead is the modern reference; 1990 Al-Maaisim Tunnel ~1,400; 2006 Jamarat Bridge ~360) and shaped global crowd-crush MCI doctrine. Mechanism: in densely-packed crowds at >5 persons/m², compressive asphyxia kills standing — victims cannot expand chest to breathe, lose consciousness while upright, then fall and are trampled. Operational lessons: (1) mass crowd-crush triage diverges from trauma triage — victims often have no external injury but compressive asphyxia, hypoxic brain injury, or rhabdomyolysis from prolonged compression; pulse + respirations checked at extraction, but rhabdomyolysis may not manifest for 6-12 hours; (2) extraction priority is to remove pressure first, then triage — pulling individuals out of crush is itself dangerous and may worsen injury; vertical extraction with body protection over the patient when possible; (3) Saudi Hajj Health Ministry deploys ~30,000 health personnel + 25 hospitals + 150 ambulances during Hajj — pre-positioned surge that other mass gatherings cannot replicate; (4) heat stress overlay: 40-50°C ambient + crowd density compounds metabolic distress; pre-cooling stations and IV fluid surge integral to plan. Applies to: stadium events (Itaewon 2022 Korea ~159 dead, Indonesia Kanjuruhan 2022 ~135 dead), religious gatherings, music festivals.

The 29 October 2022 Itaewon Halloween crowd crush in Seoul (159 dead, mostly young adults) was a watershed for South Korean MCI command and informs urban entertainment-district crowd disaster doctrine globally. Operational lessons from Korean National Fire Agency post-incident review: (1) the alley topology (3.2m wide, 40m long, downhill) created 'crowd jam' physics — forward pressure from tens of thousands compressed people in the alley, with no escape path; modern doctrine mandates dynamic crowd density monitoring at known choke points (CCTV + AI density estimation); (2) bystander CPR overwhelmed at scene — 270+ bystanders attempted CPR on 100+ victims with no automated triage to prioritize who could still be saved; mass-casualty CPR triage (60-second pulse check, move on) is the trained protocol; (3) cellular network saturation — first emergency calls came through, subsequent calls dropped or queued, delaying full mobilization 30+ minutes; (4) hospital surge fragmented across Seoul — triage at scene to specific hospitals (rather than nearest) prevents single-hospital collapse; (5) post-incident mental health surge for survivors, bystanders, first responders — Seoul Metropolitan Government extended trauma counseling 12 months. EmergencyNet relevance: Meshtastic mesh remains operational when cellular saturated — directly addresses Itaewon-mode failure.

The 25 April 2015 Gorkha earthquake (Mw 7.8, ~9,000 dead, ~22,000 injured) and 12 May aftershock (Mw 7.3) tested USAR operations in Himalayan terrain and reshaped doctrine for high-altitude / remote-village earthquake response. Operational lessons: (1) road access denied — landslides blocked main highways; helicopter evacuation became primary, with constraints from monsoon weather (May-Sep) and altitude limiting payload; specific Mi-17 / UH-60 platforms preferred for Himalayan medevac; (2) avalanches secondary to quake at Mt Everest base camp (~22 dead) and Langtang Valley (~350 buried) — earthquake + avalanche compound mechanism produces unique injury patterns (blunt + asphyxial + hypothermic); (3) traditional earthen / mud-mortar village construction collapses differently from steel/concrete — entrapment in low-rise debris with smaller voids; lighter USAR teams (Light/Medium INSARAG typing) more effective than Heavy in these terrains; (4) cultural sensitivity in Hindu / Buddhist majority — mass cremation and burial traditions affect post-incident management; engagement with local religious authorities critical for body identification protocols; (5) altitude (1,400m+ Kathmandu, 3,000m+ remote villages) affects pulse oximeter readings — SpO2 baselines must be adjusted by altitude in triage. EmergencyNet field_ai operates well in helicopter-only access scenarios where LoRa relays from village to staging area.

The 11 March 2011 Tohoku earthquake-tsunami-Fukushima Daiichi cascade is the modern reference for combined natural disaster + radiological release MCI. Direct casualties primarily from tsunami (~18,500 dead), with radiological release affecting subsequent operations. Operational lessons relevant to civilian MCI: (1) iodine prophylaxis — KI tablets (130mg adult, weight-adjusted pediatric) within 24 hours of release reduce thyroid I-131 uptake; pre-positioned distribution at evacuation centers, not just hospitals; (2) decontamination of evacuees — surface contamination >100,000 cpm requires removal of clothing + warm water shower; ophthalmic / nasal irrigation if facial contamination; bag and seal contaminated clothing for radioactive waste protocol; (3) dose tracking — TLD badges or electronic dosimeters for first responders; ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) shapes shift rotation; cumulative dose limits drive personnel rotation more than fatigue; (4) plume modeling drives evacuation zones — wind-direction-dependent, not radius-based; geo-fence polygons should be dynamically reshaped from plume model output, not static circles; (5) long-term: cesium-137 environmental persistence affects food / water supply for years — decontamination of soil, biomass disposal protocols. EmergencyNet ZONE_RADIATION supports plume polygons (not just radius) and PPE escalation cascades.

The WHO Emergency Medical Team (EMT) Classification, formalized post-Haiti 2010 and operationalized through the EMT Coordination Cell, defines internationally-deployable medical units for sudden-onset disasters. Three tiers + specialized cells: Type 1 fixed (≥50 OPD/day, no surgery), Type 1 mobile (community outreach, rural access), Type 2 (≥100 OPD/day + 7 inpatient beds + surgery, ~20 staff), Type 3 (≥100 OPD/day + 40 inpatient beds + 2 OR + ICU, ~30+ staff including anesthetist + surgeon). Specialized: rehabilitation, infection management (Ebola), maternal/newborn, mental health. Self-sufficiency requirement: 14 days of supplies, water, power, sanitation — must not burden host country resources. Pre-deployment registration with WHO + originating country review of standards, supplies, ethics. Activation: government request through cluster system or WHO operations. Recent deployments: Türkiye-Syria 2023 (88 EMTs), Mozambique cyclone, COVID-19 surge support. Relevance for EmergencyNet: deterministic core (12-Q screening + 7 hidden risks + START/JumpSTART) is portable across EMT contexts; advisor RAG can be configured per host-country protocol set; LoRa mesh enables internal coordination in EMT field hospital where local cellular may be saturated or absent.
